serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer plate of Alici e Burrata weighs approximately 250 g, comprising burrata cheese, cured anchovies, cherry tomatoes, arugula, and a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods: Anchovy (canned in oil), Burrata/Mozzarella (whole milk), Cherry tomatoes, Arugula, Olive oil (extra virgin)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Alici e Burrata as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA SR Legacy entries for the five primary components: canned/salted anchovies (~25% by weight), burrata/whole-milk mozzarella (~35% by weight), cherry tomatoes (~20% by weight), arugula (~10% by weight), and extra virgin olive oil (~10% by weight). Input per_100g values (energy 192 kcal, protein 11.2 g, fat 15.2 g, carbohydrate 3.2 g, sodium 480 mg) are consistent with this weighted blend and are retained with medium confidence. Calcium 180 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 44 mg, saturated fat 7.2 g, trans fat 0.2 g, vitamin C 6.0 mg, dietary fibre 0.8 g, and sugar 1.6 g are also consistent with USDA component data and are retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate mixed fish and cheese dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Alici e Burrata. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on the dish's very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g), high protein (11.2 g/100 g), and high fat (15.2 g/100 g). The closest analogous reference is a low-carbohydrate mixed fish and fresh cheese dish from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.0) / 100 = 1.2 (250 g serving × 3.2 g carbohydrate/100 g = 8.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg/100 g from cured anchovies and burrata cheese
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 480 mg/100 g (1200 mg per 250 g serving) substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium limit of 600–800 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request fresh (uncured) fish in place of salted anchovies and reduce cheese quantity to lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic load (GL 1.2 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g, GL 1.2 per serving) and high protein (11.2 g/100 g) make this dish suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; however, saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g from burrata should be monitored as chronic high saturated fat intake may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from anchovies offset partially by high saturated fat (7.2 g/100 g) and high sodium (480 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Anchovies provide cardioprotective EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids and extra virgin olive oil contributes monounsaturated fats and polyphenols; however, saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g and sodium at 480 mg/100 g are cardiovascular risk factors, and cardiovascular disease patients should limit portion size and request reduced cheese and fresh rather than cured anchovies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 from anchovies and oleocanthal from olive oil, offset by pro-inflammatory saturated fat (7.2 g/100 g) and high sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Anchovies are a rich source of EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids with direct joint anti-inflammatory effects, and extra virgin olive oil provides oleocanthal (a COX inhibitor); however, saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold for RA management and cherry tomatoes (nightshade) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Alici e Burrata is a classic Italian cold appetizer (antipasto) featuring creamy burrata cheese — a fresh mozzarella shell filled with stracciatella and cream — paired with salted or oil-packed anchovies (alici), sweet cherry tomatoes, peppery arugula (rocket), and a generous drizzle of organic extra virgin olive oil. The dish is assembled rather than cooked, preserving the delicate textures of each component. Burrata contributes a rich, milky creaminess while anchovies provide an intense umami-saline contrast; arugula adds a bitter, peppery freshness and cherry tomatoes contribute acidity and sweetness. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (11.2 g/100 g) and fat (15.2 g/100 g), very low in carbohydrates (3.2 g/100 g), and has a low glycemic index (~15) and glycemic load (1.2 per 250 g serving), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and type 2 diabetes dietary patterns. Sodium is high at 480 mg/100 g due to cured anchovies, making it unsuitable for hypertension without modification. Anchovies are a notable source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. Saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g from burrata is a consideration for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and contains fish and dairy allergens. It is served as a starter in Italian restaurants and is popular in Singapore's Italian dining scene.

This is an Italian dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply as the dish is not a Singaporean dish — it is an Italian dish served in Singapore, so Chinese/Malay/Tamil/Hindi local names are not mandated by the multilingual matrix.
